Color, Accessibility, And Highlighting

Goal

This notebook focuses on the effective use of color.

Questions

  • Is there enough contrast between the important part and the background?
  • Is the legend doing too much work?
  • Would a smaller number of colors make the figure easier to read?

Load Data

state_population_raw <- readr::read_csv("data/real/state_population_ranks.csv")
Rows: 51 Columns: 4
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (2): state, state_name
dbl (2): population, rank

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

Prepare Data

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter()
Create or change columns mutate(); case_when(); if_else() 
state_population <- state_population_raw |>
  filter(rank <= 20) |>
  mutate(
    state_name = reorder(state_name, population),
    us_region = state.region[match(state, state.abb)],
    highlight_group = if_else(rank <= 5, "Largest five", "Other top 20"),
    contrast_group = case_when(
      rank <= 5 ~ "Largest five",
      rank <= 10 ~ "Next five",
      TRUE ~ "Other top 20"
    )
  )

Match Palette To Data Type

Different data types need different color jobs.

  • Qualitative palettes distinguish categories.

Helpful Color Packages

These packages will help you choose attractive, accessible colors.

  • RColorBrewer: named palettes for data visualization. Brewer palettes are useful for qualitative color scales.
  • viridis: perceptually uniform palettes that are designed to remain readable for many color-vision differences and in grayscale. ggplot2 includes viridis scale functions, so you can often use them directly.

Start with the scale functions built into ggplot2; add specialized packages when a figure needs them.

In ggplot2, you will usually use these through scale functions:

  • scale_fill_brewer() or scale_color_brewer() for discrete Brewer palettes.
Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Create or change columns mutate()
Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; fill
Geoms / stats geom_tile()
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels facet_wrap(); scale_fill_identity(); labs(); theme_void(); theme() 
palette_swatches <- tibble::tibble(
  palette = rep(
    c("RColorBrewer: Set2", "RColorBrewer: Dark2", "RColorBrewer: Blues", "ggplot2: viridis C"),
    each = 8
  ),
  step = rep(1:8, times = 4),
  color = c(
    RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(8, "Set2"),
    RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(8, "Dark2"),
    RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(8, "Blues"),
    grDevices::hcl.colors(8, "viridis")
  )
) |>
  mutate(palette = factor(palette, levels = unique(palette)))

ggplot(palette_swatches, aes(x = step, y = 1, fill = color)) +
  geom_tile() +
  facet_wrap(vars(palette), ncol = 1) +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  labs(
    title = "Color Packages Provide Tested Palettes",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold")
  )

These palettes have different jobs. Set2 and Dark2 distinguish categories. Dark2 has stronger contrast, so it is useful when categories need to stand apart.

Choose Contrasting Colors From A Palette

Small differences in color may look fine on your screen and disappear on a projector, in print, or for readers with color blindness or other vision impairments.

When you need two focus colors, choose colors that differ in hue and lightness. Here, the weak example uses two soft colors from Pastel2. The better example uses two stronger colors from Dark2. The gray context group stays quiet.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Create or change columns mutate(); case_when()
Combine data frames bind_rows() 
weak_contrast_palette <- c(
  "Largest five" = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(8, "Pastel2")[2],
  "Next five" = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(8, "Pastel2")[3],
  "Other top 20" = "#D9D9D9"
)

better_contrast_palette <- c(
  "Largest five" = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(8, "Dark2")[2],
  "Next five" = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(8, "Dark2")[3],
  "Other top 20" = "#E6E6E6"
)

contrast_examples <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  state_population |>
    mutate(
      contrast_example = "Weak contrast",
      display_color = case_when(
        contrast_group == "Largest five" ~ weak_contrast_palette[["Largest five"]],
        contrast_group == "Next five" ~ weak_contrast_palette[["Next five"]],
        TRUE ~ weak_contrast_palette[["Other top 20"]]
      )
    ),
  state_population |>
    mutate(
      contrast_example = "Better contrast",
      display_color = case_when(
        contrast_group == "Largest five" ~ better_contrast_palette[["Largest five"]],
        contrast_group == "Next five" ~ better_contrast_palette[["Next five"]],
        TRUE ~ better_contrast_palette[["Other top 20"]]
      )
    )
  ) |>
  mutate(contrast_example = factor(contrast_example, levels = c("Weak contrast", "Better contrast")))
Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; color; xend; yend
Geoms / stats geom_segment(); geom_point()
geom_segment() properties linewidth
geom_point() properties size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels facet_wrap(); scale_color_identity(); scale_x_continuous(); labs(); theme_minimal(); theme() 
ggplot(contrast_examples, aes(x = population, y = state_name, color = display_color)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = population, yend = state_name), linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(vars(contrast_example), ncol = 1) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(
    title = "Accessible Contrast Is A Design Choice",
    subtitle = "The Dark2 version separates the focus groups from each other and from the background.",
    x = "Population",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8))

Too Many Colors

Colors are not free; they bring a cognitive burden. Use color to thoughtfully convey a meaningful grouping in a way the reader can understand.

When the reader is asked to cross-reference a dozen colors, you may as well present a table. It is easier to read across a table than to scan and memorize colors.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; color
Geoms / stats geom_point()
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_continuous(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
ggplot(state_population, aes(x = population, y = state_name, color = state_name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(
    title = "Too Many Colors Make The Legend Do The Work",
    x = "Population",
    y = NULL,
    color = "State"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

This plot has accurate data, but the color system does not achieve anything.

Group Colors Into Regions

One improvement is to use color for a meaningful grouping. Here, region is more useful than state because it reduces the legend from twenty colors to four.

ggplot(state_population, aes(x = population, y = state_name, color = us_region)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(
    title = "Use Color For Meaningful Groups",
    x = "Population",
    y = NULL,
    color = "Region in the U.S."
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Reducing colors is good in principle here, but the reader still has to bounce between the plot and the legend to even make sense of the color. It is actually easier for them to just think “California is in the west” then match up the color. This is an example where color just isn’t the right tool.

Small Multiples Instead of Color

Often, faceting by small multiples is clearer than using color to indicate group membersihp. Faceting puts the regional groups into the structure of the plot, so the reader does not have to decode the colors.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y
geom_point() properties color; size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels facet_wrap(); scale_x_continuous(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
ggplot(state_population, aes(x = population, y = state_name)) +
  geom_point(color = "#005EA8", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(vars(us_region), scales = "free_y") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(
    title = "Faceting Can Be Clearer Than Color",
    x = "Population",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Here, we get the added benefit of being able to see easily how many units belong to a group as well the variation across groups. Be willing to abandon ideas for something better.

Use Contrast To Highlight

Below, we use color to highlight a certain group. This effectively draws the readers eye to a specific place in the figure.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; color; xend; yend
Geoms / stats geom_segment(); geom_point()
geom_segment() properties linewidth
geom_point() properties size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_color_manual(); scale_x_continuous(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
ggplot(state_population, aes(x = population, y = state_name, color = highlight_group)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = population, yend = state_name), linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Largest five" = better_contrast_palette[["Largest five"]],
      "Other top 20" = better_contrast_palette[["Other top 20"]]
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale_cut = scales::cut_short_scale())) +
  labs(
    title = "High Contrast Directs Attention",
    subtitle = "The largest five states stand out because the other states are muted.",
    x = "Population",
    y = NULL,
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

This is still a simple plot, but the color choices now match the reading task.

Accessibility Checks

  • Does the plot still work in grayscale?
  • Are important differences also shown by position, labels, shape, or line type?
  • Is the contrast strong enough for projection or printing?
  • Is the legend doing too much work?
  • Would direct labels or highlighting be clearer?

Takeaways

  • Use color when it carries information or directs attention.
  • Too many colors can make the legend do too much work.
  • Contrast matters: the focus color should separate from the background.
  • Do not rely on color alone; use labels, ordering, contrast, or filtering so the figure still works without color.
  • Often the best color choice is a single focus color plus a quiet background color.
